title: Structured intake form
problem: Most consulting engagements start with a 60-minute discovery call where the consultant interviews the client live. That model demands real-time scheduling, consumes hours per lead, and excludes a side-practice operator with a day job and a family. The structured intake form replaces the discovery call with a guided form a visitor fills at their own pace: they pick the shape of their problem, answer fields tuned to that shape, save a draft, and submit when ready. It is read in one batched sitting, not in real time. Because there is no AI interviewer between the visitor and the consultant — that first read of a client's pain stays human — the form itself has to be good enough to extract a usable brief from people who cannot write a brief.
analysis: A step machine where each problem-type carries its own field set, so structure is captured at intake time instead of being reverse-engineered from prose afterward. A short routing step asks what shape the problem is (paperwork, tracking, coordination, or other) and the matching field schema renders. The form auto-saves continuously: always to the browser's local storage, and — once the visitor is signed in — also to the server, so a draft started on a phone can be finished on a desktop. No AI sits between the visitor's words and the consultant's first read. Submitting either creates a real engagement record (if the visitor is already signed in) or sends a magic-link email so they can claim what they typed; either way nothing is lost. The capacity gate is enforced by the server, not the form.

decisions.decision: Schema-driven, not free-text — each problem-type has its own field set
decisions.rationale: A wall of free text forces the consultant to turn prose into a structured brief by hand. Per-type schemas extract that structure at intake time; free-text fields are kept only where structure genuinely cannot capture the nuance.
decisions.recommendation: Start with the few problem-types that match the practice's wedge and add types only when triage data shows real demand.

decisions.decision: No AI interviewer between visitor and consultant at first contact
decisions.rationale: The consultant reads every submission himself. An AI summary would put a model between him and the raw client voice — the worst version of AI-assisted consulting.
decisions.recommendation: AI may help a visitor phrase their own answer in the form, but must never replace the consultant's reading of the original text.

decisions.decision: Drafts save locally always, and to the server once signed in
decisions.rationale: A dead-end form that loses work on a refresh is a known failure mode. Local storage covers the anonymous visitor; server-side draft sync covers cross-device resume for the signed-in visitor without forcing an account before the first keystroke.
decisions.recommendation: Keep account creation lightweight — email only, magic-link, no password during the draft phase.

implementation: src/pages/Intake.tsx is a five-step machine: a vibe gate (self-qualification consent), an account step (email capture, skipped when already signed in), a type picker (four problem types), a schema-driven form step that renders the fields for the chosen type and embeds an inline napkin-sketch editor, and a confirmation screen. Step components live under src/components/intake/. The draft saves to localStorage on every keystroke; for a signed-in visitor it also debounce-syncs to the server endpoint functions/api/intake-drafts.ts (a per-email draft store) so a draft survives a device switch. On submit: a signed-in visitor's intake becomes a real engagement record via POST /api/sessions; an anonymous visitor triggers a magic-link email and their intake is held until they sign in and claim it. The capacity gate is server-side — GET /api/capacity drives a UI badge, and POST /api/sessions answers 409 when at capacity, which routes the visitor to a waitlist confirmation instead of a created engagement.
successCriteria:
- A visitor can fill the form at their own pace with no real-time scheduling
- Drafts auto-save and survive a refresh; a signed-in visitor can resume on another device
- Each problem-type renders its own field schema
- The consultant receives one structured record per submission, not free-text prose
- Submitting at capacity routes to a waitlist acknowledgement rather than failing
- Reusable as a pattern in any project that needs user-supplied structured input
userGuide: Visit /intake (or /en/intake). Step 1 is the self-qualification gate. Step 2 collects an email (skipped if you are already signed in). Step 3 picks a problem type. Step 4 is the per-type form, which auto-saves as you type and includes a sketch pad. Step 5 confirms. If you are signed in, submitting creates your engagement immediately; if not, you get a one-time sign-in link by email to claim what you typed. If the practice is at capacity, submitting puts you on the waitlist — same data, different acknowledgement.
knownLimitations: Intake messages and fields are text and sketches — no file uploads inside the form itself
Email format is checked with a regex; there is no deliverability verification
A full screen-reader and tab-order accessibility audit is still outstanding

statusHistory.reason: /intake live with the five-step flow, per-type schemas, continuous autosave, and capacity-gated submit.
Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/pages/Intake.feature.json. Re-checked the step flow, the server-side draft sync, and the magic-link submit path against the shipped code.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Initial fill from the brainstorming session — the form-as-diagnostic-instrument principle.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Built as a five-step machine with per-type schemas and local-storage drafts.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Code-first overhaul: co-located next to Intake.tsx. The runtime now exists, so the rewrite documents server-side draft sync, magic-link submit, and real engagement creation, and drops the stale 'no backend / no magic-link / no email sent' limitations.
